tagged_story_for_tts:
Ocean’s rhythmic heartbeat. Moon’s invisible pull.

The word tide comes from Old English tīd, meaning "time" or "season"—because tides arrive like clockwork, twice daily.

Most assume waves cause tides. They don’t. Tides are the Earth’s oceans bulging toward the Moon’s gravity, then sagging back as the planet spins. Imagine stretching a rubber band: the side you pull (Moon-facing) rises first; the opposite side lags, creating two bulges. The Sun’s gravity tugs too, but weaker—until it aligns with the Moon during full/new moons, making "spring tides" 20% higher.

Bay of Fundy’s tides rise 16 meters (52 ft)—taller than a 4-story building—because its funnel shape traps water. Venice’s acqua alta floods occur when tides + winds push 1.5m (5ft) surges into sinking streets.

Three related subjects are lunar orbital decay, resonance in fluid dynamics, and ancient tide prediction tools.
